Play video starting at ::13 and follow transcript
0:13
Do you support the Act of Declaration of Independence of Ukraine? That was the question that an overwhelming majority of 92.3% of Ukraine’s voters answered “yes” to on December 1st 1991. The end of 1991 was truly a new beginning for the modern Ukrainian independence from the Soviet Union. My name is Maksym Yakovlyev I am head of chair of International Relations and director of this School for Policy Analysis of the National University of Kyiv-Mohyla Academy. Chances are, that your knowledge about Ukraine is based on (and unfortunately, limited to) the depressing pictures of poverty and high levels of corruption. Well, let me fix that. Ukrainian Declaration of Independence can be viewed as a success story of a nation fighting for the cultural and historical identity, but most importantly - for the freedom Ukrainians have come a long way through foreign oppressions and & colonization efforts to finally put an end to the Soviet Union’s “prison of nations” Ukraine's history of the last three decades can be told as the history of protests, the first major political protest – the Revolution on Granite – took place on the central square of Kyiv It started in October 1990 in what was then the Ukrainian SSR. 
Play video starting at :1:38 and follow transcript
1:38
It was a protest by young people who are deeply unsatisfied with the results of the last Ukrainian parliamentary elections in which the Communist Party of Ukraine had won the majority of the parliament seats. For almost two weeks hundreds of students from all over Ukraine held the Protest on the Granite on the Maidan Square. Some of them even announced a hunger strike. The protests have resulted in the Parliament re-elections and have prompted Vitaly Masol - the chairman of the Council of Ministers of the Soviet Ukraine, to resign. Dissatisfaction with the fraudulent Presidential Elections of 2004 that were marred by massive corruption and voter intimidation, and voter intimidation led to a second outburst of protest known as the Orange Revolution. Finally, there was the Revolution of Dignity. Looking back, the history of modern Ukraine can be told the story of a post-Soviet country trying to find its place on the geopolitical map and how it tried to build a new democratic society. 
Play video starting at :2:49 and follow transcript
2:49
These two processes were always going hand-in-hand: the “multiple vectors ” diplomacy pursued by the second president of Ukraine, Leonid Kuchma, exemplifies how difficult it was to cut the old colonial ties with Russia and join the Western democratic world as a truly independent state. In the mid 1990s Ukraine started its cooperation with NATO while also trying different formats of "friendship" with Russia and the Commonwealth of Independent States led by Russia. Two decades later, the 4th President of Ukraine, Viktor Yanukovich, decided to suspend the signing of European Union Association Agreement in favor of closer cooperation with Russia and the Eurasian Economic Union. This led to a spike in protests and demonstrations in Ukraine known as Euromaidan, or the Revolution of Dignity. Ukraine put an end to the idea of multiple vectors in foreign politics, following a number of steps. Firstly, in December 2014, Verkhovna Rada renounced Ukraine's non-aligned status. Secondly, in June of 2017, it adopted legislation reinstating membership in the European Union and NATO as strategic, foreign and security policy objectives. 
Play video starting at :4:14 and follow transcript
4:14
And finally, in February of 2019, with a majority of 334 out of 385 MPs, Ukrainian Parliament voted to approve the corresponding amendments to Ukraine's Constitution. The geopolitical choice to break the colonial ties with Russia in order to make membership in the EU and NATO Ukraine's main foreign policy objectives, came at a high cost. Russia annexed Crimea and started a war against Ukraine by occupying parts of Ukrainian Donbas and creating the so-called "people's republics". The latter are not recognized by any United Nation state, not even by Russia. However, Ukrainians have proven to the world and to themselves that they are committed to human rights and democratic values as their civilizational choice and are ready to fight for them no matter what the costs are. Being a young and democratic country, Ukraine has a large historical and cultural legacy. I invite you to meet my colleagues who will guide you through the course to discover the roots of the Ukrainian nation.

The Art of Independent Ukraine and Further Reading Reading

The Art of Independent Ukraine and Further Reading

Movies:

The Tribe
 by Myroslav Slaboshpytskyi (2014).A teenage boy arrives at a boarding school for the deaf. Confronted by the violent antics of other boys and girls, he struggles to conform and join the “tribe”.

Homeward
 by Nariman Aliev (2019).A father and his son from Crimean Tatar family transport the body of a deceased older son and brother from Kyiv to bury him in Crimea.

Khaytarma
 by Akhtem Seitablaev (2019).The film portrays Crimean Tatar test pilot and Hero of the Soviet Union Amet-khan Sultan against the background of the 1944 deportation of the Crimean Tatars.

My Thoughts Are Silent
 by Antonio Lukich (2019).Vadim is twenty-two. He's a sound engineer and a downer. He is going to Canada in three months. Forever. Before the departure, he is assigned to record the voice of a rare bird, which dwells only in Transcarpathian Ukraine.

When the trees fall
 by Marysia Nikitiuk (2018).The story of a five-year-old rebellious girl Vitka, her teenage cousin Larysa, and her boyfriend, young criminal Scar, unfolds in a Ukrainian provincial setting. Larysa finds herself at a crossroads after the death of her father. Sticking to “traditions” and stereotypes, the village community ostracizes her for loving Scar.

Spell your name
 by Sergey Bukovsky (2006).Survivors tell the story of the Babi Yar massacre during World War II, when approximately 100,000 people were murdered by German forces.

Gamer
 by Oleg Sentsov (2011).The boy's name is Alex, but in the world of gamers where he spends most of his time, he is known as Koss. The enormous amount of time he spends glued to the computer screen starts to pay off. At the game clubs in a small Ukrainian village, he is an undisputed king of the shooting game Quake.

Maidan
 by Sergei Loznitsa (2014).

A look at the 2013 and 2014 civil unrest on the Ukrainian capital's central square.

My grandmother Fani Kaplan
 by Olena Demyanenko (2016).The film focuses on the little known love affair between terrorist Fanny Kaplan and Vladimir Lenin’s brother, Dmitriy Ilyich Ulyanov. Questions are raised in the movie whether Kaplan really tried to assassinate Lenin in 1918.

Cherkasy
 by Tymur Yashchenko (2019).The crew of Ukrainian Navy minesweeper U311 "Cherkasy" is resisting seizure of the vessel by Russian army in Crimea in 2014.

Books:

The White Chalk of Days: The Contemporary Ukrainian Literature Series Anthology by Mark Andryczyk (editor). The anthology presents a translation of literary works by Series guests that imaginatively describe pivotal issues in today’s Ukraine and express its tribulations and jubilations. Featuring poetry, fiction, and essays by fifteen Ukrainian writers, the anthology offers English-speaking readers a wide array of the most beguiling literature written in Ukraine in the past fifty years.

Fieldwork in Ukrainian Sex by Oksana Zabuzhko.The novel is narrated in first person by a sharp-tongued author with a strong and honest voice. She is a visiting professor of Slavic Studies at Harvard, and her exposure to American values and behaviors is combined with her yearning to break free from Ukrainian conventions. In her despair over a recently ended affair, she turns her attention to the details of her lover’s abusive behavior. Detailing the power her Ukrainian lover wielded over her and admitting the underlying reasons for her attraction to him, she begins to see the concepts that define her as a Ukrainian woman. Doing so, she exposes and calls those concepts into question.

Voroshilovgrad by Serhiy Zhadan. A city-dwelling executive heads home to take over his brother's gas station after the latter’s mysterious disappearance, but he only finds mysteries and ghosts at home. The bleak industrial landscape of now-war-torn eastern Ukraine sets the stage for Voroshilovgrad, the Soviet era name of the Ukrainian city of Luhansk, mixing magical realism and exhilarating road novel in poetic, powerful, and expressive prose.

Tango of Death by Yuri Vynnychuk. Yuri Vynnychuk’s novel Tango of Death is a literary masterpiece about the magic of pre-war Lviv.

Maybe Esther: A Family Story by Katja Petrowskaja.An inventive, unique, and extraordinarily debut memoir that brings together the fascinating story of one woman’s family across twentieth-century Russia, Ukraine, Poland, and Germany.

The UnSimple
 by Taras Prokhasko. 
The book tells the story of the small town of Ialivets and the mountainous region surrounding it. It weaves history with folklore and fantasy, politics with poetry, and ethnology with botany and philosophy. Yet, the resulting text is striking because of its simple beauty. This is a work of magical realism saturated with the distinct, melancholic, and yet ironic poetics of Central Europe, a story that is both hyper-local and yet universally accessible, and is one of the finest works by one of Ukraine’s most enthralling writers. 

Sweet Darusya: A Tale of Two Villages by Maria Matios. The book reveals a family saga that is much more dynamic than classical sagas and at the same time much more touching and engaging. It is an emotional history of Ukraine with a well-researched and vivid historical background that gives the reader an opportunity to understand not only the characters and their worries but the entire drama of the country/countries in which they lived without leaving their village.

Life Went on Anyway: Stories by Oleg Sentsov.The stories written by Ukrainian film director, writer, and dissident Oleg Sentsovseemingly stem from his opposition to Russia's invasion and occupation of eastern Ukraine. Sentsov was sentenced to 20 years in prison on spurious terrorism charges in August 2015 after he was kidnapped from his house and put through a grossly unfair trial by the Russian military court.

Recreations by Yuri Andrukhovych and Marko Pavlyshyn. Recreations is a novel of carnivalesque vitality and acute social criticism. It celebrates the newly found freedom and reflects upon the contradictions of post-Soviet society.

Death and the Penguin by Andrey Kurkov. Victor is depressed: his lover has dumped him, his short stories are too short, and the light has gone off in his dingy apartment. His only companion is Misha, the penguin he rescued from Kyiv Zoo, when it could not feed the animals anymore. Misha is a silent witness to Victor's despair who joins his celebration consisting of fish and vodka when Victor's luck seems to turn. He is commissioned to write obituaries. The weird thing is that the editor wants him to select subjects who are still alive and who are the movers and shakers of the new post-Communist society.

items: Rus' as an Early Medieval Kingdom

Play video starting at ::13 and follow transcript
0:13
The history of the Ukrainian lands can be begun with Kimmerian and Scythian tribes near the Sea of Azov or the Black Sea. It can also be begun with the first Slavic people settling all over South Eastern Europe. But we are going to begin our story with the land of Rus from IX century. My name is Vadym Aristov I am a historian of the Middle Ages and I will walk you through Medieval times in Ukraine. So, what was Rus? It was a Medieval polity of Eastern Europe. Its heartland was located in Northern Ukraine with Kyiv as its main political and religious center Rus was similar to other European Early Medieval kingdoms, such as the kingdoms of Franks, Visigoths, Anglo-Saxons, Langobards As a rule, such 'kingdoms' emerged as a combination of local majority dominated by migrant minority. 
Play video starting at :1:13 and follow transcript
1:13
Rus was inspired by the Viking Age which had transformed Northern and Eastern Europe. The discovery of trading routes to the Middle East and the demographic boom in Scandinavia led to a gradual expansion of the North Germanic population. Their westward movement became known as the Viking raids. At the same time, the Norsemen who moved eastward were called the Rus. The Norsemen-Rus’ targets were the rich markets of Muslim countries and Byzantium They sold slaves and fur in exchange for silver coins and silk clothes. Eastern Europe served as a resource base for Rus traders At the end of the 9th century, one of their groups settled in the Middle Dnipro. Kyiv emerged as the headquarters of the Dnipro Rus which specialized in trade with Byzantium. Similar to Western Vikings, the Norsemen-Rus raided the Byzantine lands and the Caspian Sea shores Nevertheless, they preferred trading over raiding. This small but powerful community of the Norsemen Rus made the local Slavic people tributaries and later conquered them The foundation of Rus as a polity in the late 10th century is associated with one of the leaders of the Norsemen-Rus - Volodymyr He was baptized after the capture of the Byzantine city Cherson in Crimea in 988 Being a barbarian, he married the sister of the Emperor, which was an exception in the Byzantine practice. 
Play video starting at :2:55 and follow transcript
2:55
This event had marked the radical change. The leaders of Dnipro Rus became territorial rulers. A network of new towns and fortresses started to expand. A new society was born. Volodymyr's descendants had a monopoly on political power in their realm. They constituted the highest aristocracy in Ruthenian society. The Rurikids - as the clan is called today - controlled the territory of Northern and Western Ukraine as their domain and also the territories of today's Belarus and the Western regions of Russia as peripheral provinces. 
Play video starting at :3:33 and follow transcript
3:33
Being a Christian royal family, the Rurikids became closely related with European dynasties through marriages. Volodymyr’s son Yaroslav married Swedish princess Three of his sons were married to Polish, German and Byzantine princesses. Three daughters of Yaroslav became wifes of the Norwegian, French and Hungarian kings. Searching for a better place in the dynastic hierarchy and more prestigious lands, Ruthenian princes often changed their thrones and moved around throughout their lives. There are constantly competing over lands and towns. Kyiv, the oldest city and the highest throne, was their grand prize. With the increase in the number of the Rurikids' in the 12th century, the family split into separate clans. 
Play video starting at :4:27 and follow transcript
4:27
Each of them occupied a certain territory in the different regions of Eastern Europe. In the early 13th century. Ukrainian territories were controlled by three powerful royal clans who held the Left-Bank Dnipro, Right-Bank Dnipro and Volhynia The Rurikids have become a heart of the medieval Ukrainian society. Military elite which was transforming landowners, influential clergy, rich and active townspeople, numerous and still personally free peasants. All these key elements of medieval social order were present here. Administrative structures were developed as the royal power was expanded. Rus was on the way to overcome initial political fragmentation and consolidate into a more stable state-like institution. 
Play video starting at :5:21 and follow transcript
5:21
This process was disrupted by the catastrophic Mongol invasion of 1239-1241. Most of the Ruthenian cities in Ukraine (including Kyiv) and fortified settlements, were destroyed. Rus suffered heavy human losses. After that, most of Ukrainian lands became incorporated over subordinated to the Mongol Empire. However, the traditional social order, political structures and culture prevailed in Western Ukraine. The Volhynian branch of the Rurikids retained control over Volhynia and Galicia. In spite of the vassal position towards Mongols, they strove for regional leadership and became closely integrated with their Western neighbors. 
Play video starting at :6:5 and follow transcript
6:05
In 1253, Prince Danylo was crowned by the legate of the Pope This event was symbolically important. The Orthodox Prince was distinguished with the title of Catholic rulers. In the first half of the 14th century the last male representatives of the Volynian royal dynasty passed away. After that, the Ukrainian (Ruthenian) lands were inherited by the Rurikids’ closest relatives - the Gediminids, the dynasty of Lithuanian origin, and the king of Poland. 
: Added to Selection. Press [CTRL + S] to save as a note

Glossary and Further Readings

Volodymyr was a medieval Kyivan Rus’ ruler, Grand Prince of Kyiv (980-1015), who consolidated and Christianized Rus’. In the Ukrainian tradition, Volodymyr is revered as a saint and considered equal to apostles.

988 — Christianization of Kyivan Rus’. In 988, Grand Prince Volodymyr captured the city of Cherson in Crimea. Following that victory, he was baptized. In the same or next year, the people of Kyiv were baptized in the Dnieper River. This act led to the Christianization of the whole country.

Volodymyr married Anna, the sister of the Byzantine emperor. It was prohibited for Byzantium princesses to marry barbarians and even Christian rulers. However, in 988, the emperor critically needed military assistance to defend his throne from the rebels approaching the capital. Thus, he appealed for help to Volodymyr who had just captured Cherson. In his turn, Volodymyr requested Anna’s hand and promised to convert to Christianity. In such a situation, an exception could be made for the Kyivan Rus’ ruler. After Anna was sent to him, Volodymyr sent 6000 warriors to the emperor. The rebels were defeated soon. Thus, Volodymyr's extraordinary marriage was the result of a political agreement.

The Rurikids. According to the Primary Chronicle, Varangian leader Rurik who came to Eastern Europe was the legendary ancestor of the Rus’ royal dynasty. However, this legend is probably a fiction. Although we conventionally call the dynasty the Rurikids, such a name is absent in Medieval sources.

nodes.rationale: Introducing the Kimmerian and Scythian tribes at this point provides a foundational understanding of the early inhabitants of the Ukrainian region, setting the stage for subsequent cultural and historical developments.

nodes.text: Sea of Azov or the Black Sea
nodes.rationale: The Sea of Azov and the Black Sea are introduced here to highlight their historical significance as key geographical features that shaped trade, interactions, and the settlement patterns of early tribes like the Kimmerians and Scythians.

The introduction of Slavic people is crucial for understanding the ethnolinguistic changes in Ukrainian history, providing context to the cultural and demographic evolution from ancient tribes to the formation of Kyivan Rus'.
Discussing the concept of Ukrainian lands is essential to establish the geographical and cultural continuum that has defined Ukraine's historical territory through various periods and influences.

Examining Rus early in the course allows students to explore the origins of the medieval polity that became a major cultural and political foundation for Ukraine, linking past and present historical narratives.
Positioning the Middle Ages in the course provides a chronological framework to explore the significant transformations in governance, society, and culture that affected Ukraine during this era.

Kyiv is introduced as a focal concept to underscore its role as a cultural, religious, and political center in Ukrainian history, vital for understanding its historical centrality since ancient times.

The concept of polity is introduced to examine the development of structured governance and the emergence of early political entities in the Ukrainian territories, crucial for tracing political evolution.

These groups are introduced to provide comparative insights into parallel developments in Europe, illustrating the broader context in which Ukrainian history unfolded during similar migratory and settlement patterns.

Introducing the Viking Age contextualizes the era of major trade and cultural exchanges that influenced the formation and expansion of Kyivan Rus, highlighting its connections to broader European history.

nodes.text: Trading Routes to the Middle East
nodes.rationale: The concept is introduced to emphasize the strategic importance of Ukraine's location in connecting Europe with the Middle East, influencing its economic and cultural development.
This concept is introduced to discuss the shift in priorities and strategies among emerging polities, reflecting a pivot towards sustainable economic growth and long-term stability.
Establishing Eastern Europe as a concept helps students understand the regional dynamics and influences that shaped Ukraine's historical trajectory within a larger geographical and political context.

Exploring Volodymyr's role and impact is essential to understanding the political and religious changes during his reign, particularly the Christianization efforts pivotal to the evolution of Kyivan Rus'.
Introducing the Rurikids is critical for examining the dynastic lineage that shaped the ruling structures and territorial expansion of Kyivan Rus, highlighting internal and external challenges.
Ruthenian Society is covered to explore the social fabric and cultural identity of the people during the Kyivan Rus era, providing insights into societal organization and daily life.
Focusing on dynastic competition reveals the internal power struggles that affected stability and continuity in Kyivan Rus, making it essential for understanding political dynamics and fragmentation.
Introducing Kyiv as a prize concept illustrates the city's strategic and symbolic importance, serving as a focal point for control and influence in the region's historical power plays.

The Mongol invasion is a pivotal event that reshaped the political and social landscape of Ukraine, making it crucial to comprehend the shift in power dynamics and regional stability after this period.
Examining this branch highlights regional governance and political fragmentation following the Mongol invasions, providing insights into the shifts in territorial control and influence.
This concept is introduced to explore the strategies and influences of Western interactions, contributing to the diversification of cultural and political developments in Ukraine over time.
Christianization of Kyivan Rus is examined to understand the transformative impact of religious conversion on societal structure, state consolidation, and long-term cultural identity.

Play video starting at ::12 and follow transcript
0:12
Ruth adopted Christianity from Byzantium. It became a province of constant in Nepal it and patriarchy and cave became the residents of the metropolitan of Ruth. The newly born Ruthenian church embraced all the lubricates territories and all of their Christian subordinates. In 1054, Christianity split into two divisions, Catholic and Orthodox. Formerly Ruth became a part of orthodox Europe. Yet there was no clear boundary between Ruth and it's Catholic neighbors. Only after the capture of Constantinople by the Crusaders in 1204, there has been a change of the attitude between Catholics and Orthodox people. 
Play video starting at ::55 and follow transcript
0:55
Ruthenian started to be seen as heretics by the authorities of the Roman Church. Since the 13th century, the religious question was a major factor in relations between Ruth and its Western neighbors. Christianity brought literacy tools. Unlike Christianity, the alphabet was brought by the South Slavic countries, Bulgaria in particular. In the late 19th century, the Cyrillic alphabet was invented for newly converted Slavs by the disciples of Cyril and Methodius, the Apostles of Slavs. 100 years later, it was adopted in Ruth along with the literary language, old church Slavonic, which was based on Macedonian and Bulgaria dialects. For lieutenants who spoke proudly Ukrainian dialects, this language was understandable but still foreign. Old Church Slavonic was used primarily for church needs. 
Play video starting at :1:50 and follow transcript
1:50
This was the language for the biblical texts and services. However, the literary language rapidly began to be used outside the sacral sphere. There is evidence of the spread of literacy since the mid 11th century. Numerous graffiti on church walls and birch bark manuscripts discovered by archaeologists indicate a comparatively higher literacy rate among the city population. At the time, literacy came into the legislature. Since the 11th century, written law had appeared in the form of the collection of judicial instructions. It is known as Ruthenian justice or Ruthenian law. 
Play video starting at :2:30 and follow transcript
2:30
Almost every early Medieval Barbarian kingdom had similar codices based on the case law system. The Salian law of the Franks, Frisian law, Bavarian law, Saxon law and others. Most of the books circulating in [inaudible] Ruth for a corpus of our translations from the Bulgarian or Byzantine text. However, the original Ruthenian and literature also developed. One of the most interesting works is the pilgrimage of Daniel, the story of an abbot who visited the Holy Land in the times of the Crusades. In the early 11th century, Ruthenian started to put down their history. Historical writing in medieval Ukraine evolved in the form of animals and Chronicles, similar to many other European countries. 
Play video starting at :3:20 and follow transcript
3:20
The first Ruthenium Chronicle, which is called the Primary Chronicle, was composed in the early 12th century. It began with the legendary history of Slavs and Druze and then narrated and Morrisons and accurate history. The Primary Chronicle was similar to such basic European historical works as Ecclesiastical History of the English people, by Bede, history of the France, by Gregory of Tours, or chronicle of Bohemia by Cosmos of Prague. The chronicles and Daniel's were written predominantly by the clergy. Nevertheless, the Ruthenian princes were also literate, at least some of them. In the early 12th century, Volodymyr Monomakh, Prince of Kyiv, composed the instruction to his children. He gave advice on how to rule effectively and provided his short biography as an example of good ruler shape.

Glossary and Further Readings

The Rurikids and European dynasties. Volodymyr’s son Yaroslav married Swedish princess Ingegerd. Three of his sons were married to Polish, German, and Byzantine princesses. Yaroslav’s three daughters (Elisiv, Anne, and Anastasia) became wives of the Norwegian, French, and Hungarian kings respectively. The last Anglo-Saxon king Harold Godwinson's daughter Gytha became the wife of Kyivan Rus’ Prince Volodymyr Monomakh before 1076. Ruthenian Princess Eupraxia was an Empress of the Holy Roman Empire as a wife of Emperor Henry IV. Henry IV married Eupraxia in 1089. However, Eupraxia later accused Henry of ill-treating her and forcing her to participate in orgies. She escaped to Italy where she sought help from the Pope. Finally, she left Henry IV and returned to Kyiv.

Prince Danylo. In 1253, Prince Danylo was crowned by the legate of Pope Innocent IV. This event was symbolic. The Orthodox prince was distinguished with the title of Catholic rulers.

0:13
Ukraine entered the early modern period as a part of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, united Poland and Lithuania. For Ukraine, as for the rest of Europe, the 16th and 17th in centuries were times of religious debate, war and cultural uprising. We are going to: find out who the Cossacks were and how they changed the political map of Ukrainian land; explore the emergence of a new Christian confession; and look at the roots of Ukrainian universities. All of this, we are going to explore in the third module of our course. My name is Tetyana Grygorieva, I am an Early-Modern historian and an Associate Professor at the National University of Kyiv-Mohyla Academy. It is my pleasure to invite you to Early-Modern Ukraine. So let's begin. In Western Europe, the famous demarche of Martin Luther with his 95 theses led to several outcomes the split of the Catholic community, the emancipation of several European monarchs from the spiritual authority of the Pope, harsh confessional conflicts, and, ultimately, the Reformation of the Catholic church. 
Play video starting at :1:20 and follow transcript
1:20
So, on one hand, the Reformation meant the emergence of Lutheran, Calvinist, Zwinglian and several other confessions, the war of rebellious kurfürsts with the Emperor Charles V, religious wars in France, and, finally, the Thirty Years’ War, where most of military unions were concluded between members of the same concessions. On the other hand, though, the concept of Reformation meant the reform of the Catholic church in the course of the Council of Trent, through various administrative and disciplinary measures such as unification of liturgical practices and prayer books. For a long time, all these processes had little impact on Ukraine, for several reasons. First, the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth was renowned for its policy of religious tolerance. Second, In Ukrainian lands, Orthodoxy was the dominant religion, practiced almost exclusively. Nevertheless, both of the characteristic features of the Reformation – namely, confessional conflict and church reform, have found their way into Ukrainian history. The religious situation for Ukraine was about to change at the end of the 16th century. 
Play video starting at :2:35 and follow transcript
2:35
King Sigismund III initiated the creation of the so-called Uniate church. This Uniate church was effectively the Orthodox church on the territory of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, retaining its organization and liturgical practices, yet subordinating not to the Patriarch of Constantinople, but to the Pope. from then on. This decision, which was manifested in the Union of Brest (1596) and supported by most Orthodox bishops, led to extensive confessional conflict in Ukraine. In Ukraine, the real power was held by Ruthenian princes, who claimed to be the descendants of the princes of medieval Kyivan Rus. The most prominent princely family Ostrogsky – strongly opposed the idea of the Uniate church Thus, even though the authority of the Orthodox church had been proclaimed null and void, it continued to exist under the patronage of the Ruthenian princes Yet, despite numerous religious disputes between Uniate and Orthodox theologists and occasional conflicts regarding confessional membership of particular churches and monasteries, the confessional conflict in Ukraine never achieved the level of that between the Catholics and the Protestants in Western Europe. The Orthodox church in Ukraine officially regained its legal status only in 1632 under the new king Wladyslaw IV. The new Kyivan Metropolitan was Petro Mohyla – or in the Ukrainian pronunciation, Petro Mohyla – the son of the former Moldavian hospodar and a Transylvanian princess. Petro Mohyla was educated in Lviv school. and later, according to different scholars, either at the Academy in Zamostya or in Sorbonne in Paris. 
Play video starting at :4:20 and follow transcript
4:20
It is Petro Mohyla who is credited for the reform of the Orthodox Church in the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth. Specifically, he initiated the creation of the Orthodox Creed, which defined the fundamentals of the Orthodox confession and was later approved by all the four Orthodox patriarchs. He conducted administrative reform of church provinces, establish the ecclesiastic court, codified religious ceremonies and reorganized the printing press in Kyiv to supply the clergy with all the liturgical texts they needed. Mohyla also initiated large-scale renovation of churches and monasteries in Kyiv to help the city regain its reputation as an Orthodox religious center. Finally, Mohyla united the two existing schools in Kyiv into a collegium, following the model of Jesuit colleges. The students of the collegium were taught Ruthenian, Polish, Greek and Latin, as well as having classes at the level of studia humaniora. At the beginning of the 18th century. this collegium was the first in Eastern Europe to gain the status of Academy. In the 20th century it gave its name to one of the top Ukrainian universities, the National University of Kyiv-Mohyla Academy, where I work. 
Play video starting at :5:38 and follow transcript
5:38
Altogether, Mohyla’s fame as the true reformer of Kyivan Orthodoxy comes from his Ortodox church reform modeled after the Catholic Reformation in Western Europe and his reinnervation and educatio

Glossary and Further Readings

Ukraine in the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth. By the middle of the 14th century, Ukrainian territories were divided between the Grand Duchy of Lithuania and the Kingdom of Poland. The Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth was established by the Union of Lublin in July 1569. Effectively, it was a dynastic union, which presupposed the common elective monarch as well as common customs borders. The monarch simultaneously held the titles of the King of Poland and the Grand Duke of Lithuania. However, Poland and Lithuania retained their own laws, armies, treasuries, and languages of administration. The Ukrainian lands (voivodeships), which formerly belonged to the Grand Duchy of Lithuania, were transferred to the Kingdom of Poland, yet being granted a large degree of administrative autonomy.

The Uniate Church and the Union of Brest (1596). The Union of Brest was a church agreement intended to bring Ukrainian (Ruthenian) Orthodox Christians under spiritual supremacy of the Roman Catholic Church. This move was supposed to establish loyalty of the Orthodox subjects to the Catholic monarch of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth as well as to reject claims of the neighboring Orthodox monarch, the Muscovite Tsar, for the Ukrainian lands as his ‘historical ancestry’. The metropolitan of Kyiv and the majority of bishops signed the act of this church union with Rome. Having recognized papal supremacy, the Uniate Church effectively retained Orthodox rites and the Slavonic liturgical language as well as its administrative autonomy and traditional discipline, including married clergy.

Ruthenian princes (they claimed their origin either from the princes of the medieval Old Rus’ or from the medieval grand dukes of Lithuania): Ostrogski, Korecki, Czartoryski, Wiśniowiecki, Zbaraski, Sangushko, Chetvertenski, Zaslavski, etc.

Petro Mohyla — Orthodox monk and theologian of Moldavian origin who served as metropolitan of Kyiv in 1633-1647. Perto Mohyla authored the Orthodox Creed (The Orthodox Confession of Faith) summarizing the fundamentals of the Orthodox doctrine.

As a metropolitan, Mohyla exerted significant efforts to improve the education of the Orthodox clergy and laity at the time when both Roman Catholic and Protestant missionaries were active in the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth. Mohyla also obtained the Polish monarch’s acknowledgement of the rights of the Orthodox church on the territory of the Commonwealth. In 1632, he united the Kyiv Brotherhood School and Kyiv Pechersk Lavra school into a single school of humanities and theology and invited Western-trained faculty. The school gained the status of academy when Ukraine was part of the Russian Empire. The academy had an enormous impact on the intellectual life of the Empire, and its graduates took the highest state positions as well as formed the faculty of the newly-founded St. Petersburg University.

Academy in Zamostya was founded by Grand Crown Chancellor and Grand Crown Hetman Jan Zamoyski in the town of Zamostya in 1594. It was the third academy founded in the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth (the other two were in Krakow and in Wilno).

Studia humaniora - the educational level in schools modelled upon Jesuit colleges, which included grammar classes (infima, grammar, syntax) studied for 3-4 years and humanistic classes (poetics, rhetoric) generally studied for 3 years.

This serves as a foundation to explore how these changes shaped Ukrainian identity and culture.

Introducing the Cossacks allows students to explore a distinct Ukrainian socio-political group that played a crucial role in the regional power dynamics and cultural identity, particularly in the context of their relationship with the Orthodox Church and their impact on Ukraine’s struggle for autonomy.

The Orthodox Creed is introduced as part of the detailed exploration of the Reform of the Orthodox Church to highlight its doctrinal evolution and the efforts made towards consolidating religious identity, essential for students to grasp the complexities of religious transformations within Ukraine.

items: A New Military Force in Europe: the Ukrainian Cossacks

Play video starting at ::13 and follow transcript
0:13
In the 16th century, the territory of Ukraine was divided between the Grand Duchy of Lithuania and the Kingdom of Poland. After 1569, both parts were reunited within the single Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth. On the south-eastern border. Ukrainian lands were threatened by the raids from the Crimean Khanate, which saw the territory as an important resource base for its Black Sea slave trade. The steppe areas bordering the Crimean Khanate saw the emergence of a specific frontier community – the Ukrainian Cossacks Similar frontier communities could be found in the lands neighboring the Ottoman Empire – e.g. uskoks in Croatia, hajduks in Serbia, or Don Cossacks in Muscovy. Out of all of them, however, the Ukrainian Cossacks achieved unprecedented influence. Most of our knowledge about this community comes from the descriptions by foreign observers such as Habsburg ambassador Erich Lasota von Steblau or French engineer Guillaume Le Vasseur de Beauplan The former arrived to recruit Cossacks for the service of the Habsburg Emperor during the Long War (1591-1606) with the Ottoman Empire and the latter was working on the construction of the border fortifications at the south east frontier of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth From these descriptions, we know about the multiethnic and multireligious composition of the Cossack community, the organization of the Cossacks settlement (Sich), and its administration, military capacity, democratic decision making, customs and medicine. 
Play video starting at :1:54 and follow transcript
1:54
The border governor of Kaniv and Cherkasy, prince Dmytro Bajda Vyshnevetsky, is credited for founding the legendary private fortress on the Khortytsia island meant for defending his lands from Tartar raids. This fortress became the third centre of the emerging Cossack community. Polish king-elect of Transylvanian origin Stefan Bathory, who recruited some of the Cossacks for state service and included them in the "payment register". These Cossacks became known as ‘registry Cossacks’. Bathory’s move was fully in line with the ongoing military revolution in Europe, where mercenary infantry was increasingly winning over cavalry knights. The ‘registry Cossacks’ received regular pay, were exempt from taxes, gained the privileges of self-government and self-jurisdiction, and were provided with a place for their arsenal and hospital: the town of Trachtemyriv Over the second half of the 16th century and the first half of the 17th, the number of Cossacks included in that registry fluctuated from 500 to 8000 people. They served to withstand the raids from the Crimean Khanate and were engaged in the wars the Commonwealth led against Muscovy and Sweden. The number of Cossacks actually inhabiting the south-eastern border area vastly outnumbered those included on the register, though. For example, during the Battle Khotyn (1621), when the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth withstood the attack of the Ottoman Empire, Cossack hetman Petro Sahajdachny is reported to have brought 40,000 Cossacks with him to help the Polish army In the last quarter of the 16th century, the out-of-register Cossacks took sides in internal conflicts in Moldavia. 
Play video starting at :3:49 and follow transcript
3:49
They were recruited by the Habsburgs to participate in the Thirty Years’ War. In the 1630s, the Cossacks supported the Crimean khan. Inayet Geray in his fight for independence against the Ottoman Empire. The Cossacks’ involvement in military conflicts in Muscovy, Sweden, Crimea, Moldavia, Silesia and Luxembourg proved them to be a powerful force, making an impact on central Eu

Glossary and Further Readings

Crimean Khanate — a Nomadic state which controlled the Crimean Peninsula, the southern steppe of inland Ukraine, and the regions of Kuban and southern Caucasus from approximately 1430 to 1783. The Crimean Khanate was one of the successors of the disintegrated Golden Horde. Its ruling dynasty, the Girays, claimed their origin from the elder son of legendary Mongol Emperor Genghis Khan. In Western European sources, the Crimean Khanate is often referred to as Little Tartary in contrast to Grand Tartary, i.e., the Mongol Empire. After 1478, Crimean khans acknowledged subordination to the Ottoman sultans and provided military help during Ottoman military campaigns. Yet, they maintained their own diplomatic relations with the neighboring Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth and the Ukrainian Cossacks. After 1783, the Crimean Khanate became subordinated to the Russian Empire.

Sich — an administrative and military center of the Ukrainian Cossacks. Various contemporary observers and writers describe Sich differently, yet one may refer to it as kind of a fortified military camp. In contemporary sources, it is also mentioned as Zaporozhian Sich (i.e., fortifications located down the rapids of the Dnipro river). During the 16th-18th centuries, the Sich changed its location and appearance seven or eight times. The last Sich was destroyed in 1775 by the Russian army returning from the Russian-Ottoman war.

Prince Dmytro Baida Vyshnevetsky (d. 1563) — the first Cossack chieftain credited for founding a fortress on the Khortytsia Island, which became the center of the emerging Cossack community and a prototype of the future Cossack settlements (Sich). Vyshnevetsky descended from the mighty Ruthenian noble family, and in the 1550s, he was the appointed border governor in the towns of Cherkasy and Kaniv. At different periods, as a military leader of private regiments, which included the Cossacks, Vyshnevetsky provided military service not only to the Polish king but also to the Muscovite tsar and the Ottoman sultan. In 1563, during a military campaign to Moldavia, where Vyshnevetsky intended to claim the title of Moldavian hospodar, he was defeated, taken prisoner to Istanbul, and executed.

Registered Cossacks — the Cossacks who were accepted into special Cossack units of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth army, enrolled in a particular register, and provided with regular payment for their military services. The first effort to secure the Cossacks for military service at the southern borders of the country was made in 1524 by Polish King Sigismund I the Old (1506-1548). However, this practice was properly institutionalized only under King Stephen Bathory (1576-1586). Registered Cossacks collaborated with the Cossacks of Zaporizhzhya, yet they had different military leadership.

The Khotyn War — military actions during the siege of the Khotyn fortress in northern Bessarabia by the Ottoman army in August-October, 1621. The fortress was defended by the joint armies of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth and the Cossacks. Unsuccessful besiegement of the fortress persuaded Sultan Osman II to negotiate peace with the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth. As there are well-grounded estimates that the siege of Khotyn was planned as the beginning of the large-scale campaign aimed at the Polish capital of Cracow or the Habsburg capital of Vienna, this peace was celebrated in Europe as the grand victory of Christendom.

Petro Konashevych-Sahaidachny — hetman of the Zaporozhian Sich in 1616-1622 (intermittently). Sahaidachny descended from the Ruthenian noble family in Galicia and graduated from the Ostroh Academy. At the beginning of the 17th century, he decided to join the Cossacks of Zaporizhzhia, won respect and recognition for his courage and military talent, and was subsequently elected as the chieftain of the Zaporozhian Cossacks and then as the hetman of the registered Cossacks. At the same time, Sahaidachny closely collaborated with the leading Orthodox churchmen. In 1620, his support helped persuade Patriarch of Jerusalem Theophanes to establish the new Kyivan Metropolitan for the Orthodox church (declared illegal after the Union of Brest (1595)) and to restore the Orthodox church hierarchy. In 1621, Sahaidachny led the Cossack army, which played a key role in defending the fortress of Khotyn against the Ottoman forces.

items: The State-Making Experience of the Ukrainian Cossacks

Some screen readers may require using CTRL in conjunction with the alt key

Play video starting at ::13 and follow transcript
0:13
All Cossacks – whether they were paid and included in the registry or not – gradually started to consider themselves as a rather privileged community. There were numerous rebels among the Cossacks in the beginning of the 17th century, who demand that the expansion of the register and wanted to defend their rights, they believed they deserved as military men. In 1648, charismatic Cossack hetman Bohdan Khmelnytsky turned this uprising into a large-scale rebellion, later called the Cossack Revolution. Importantly, Khmelnytsky secured the help of the Crimean Khan Islam III Geray from the very beginning. The first impressive victories of united Cossack & Crimean – near Zhovti Vody, Korsun’, Pyliavtsi and Zbarazh – proved them to be a serious challenge to the crown army. On December 27th, 1648 Khmelnytsky triumphantly entered Kyiv He was greeted by the members of the Orthododx Church elite, led by the Metropolitan of Kyiv, Sylvestr Kosiv. This solemn reception cemented Khmelnytsky as the leader of the entire Orthodox community of the Polish Lithuanian Commonwealth. 
Play video starting at :1:30 and follow transcript
1:30
The Treaty of Zboriv (1648) with the Polish king formally granted the Cossacks full control of three voivodeships Kyiv, Chernihiv and Pereyaslav though after their defeat near Berestechko (1651), their control was limited to the voivodeship of Kyiv only Thus, Khmelnytsky and his colonels made Ukrainian Cossacks the only frontier community in Central Eastern Europe to have developed into an autonomous political and territorial entity. Even though Khmelnytsky was formally appointed as a governor by the Polish king, he behaved as an independent ruler. He established diplomatic relations with the neighboring principalities of Moldavia and Wallachia, Transylvania Muscovy, and the Ottoman Empire. After Khmelnytsky lost the support of the Crimean Khanate he sought protection from the Tsar of Muscovy, Alexei Mikhailovich. In 1654, this protection was manifested in the Treaty of Pereyaslav. However, the parties, Khmelnytsky and the Tsar, understood the treaty differently. Let me explain why. Khmelnytsky was used to the strong parliamentary tradition of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth. There, elected kings signed political contracts — pacta conventa — manifesting their royal obligations to the nobility. 
Play video starting at :2:54 and follow transcript
2:54
So Khmelnytsky perceived the treaty as a political contract between the Tsar and himself, where each party enjoyed its own rights and obligations. The Tsar, on the other hand, interpreted it as a unilateral act of his own will mandating unconditional incorporation of Ukraine into the Tsardom of Muscovy. This misunderstanding served as the reason for a number of conflicts between Cossack hetmans and Russian tsars over the second half of the 17th century and led to the Polish-Moscow war (1654-1667) As a result of the war, Ukraine was divided along the river Dnipro by the two rivals. Ultimately, the controversy between tsars and hetmans culminated in the conflict between the Hetman Ivan Mazepa and the Tsar, Peter the Great. Well-educated Mazepa strived for the wide autonomy of his hetman office and maintained an exquisite court in his capital of Baturyn. While visiting the court, the ambassador from the French king Louis XV Jean de Baluze, noted that Mazepa apologized for his poor French, and spoke Polish and Latin to him while addressing his doctors in German and his architects in Italian. Mazepa, resentful of Peter the Great for neglecting his ‘obligations’ to the Cossacks, supported the tsar’s rival in the Great Northern war – the Swedish king Charles XII. 
Play video starting at :4:24 and follow transcript
4:24
Unfortunately for Mazepa, the combined Cossack and Swedish army lost to Peter the Great in the Battle of Poltava (1709) , which virtually put an end to the disputes about the autonomous status of the Ukrainian Cossacks within Russia. Hetman Mazepa fled to the Ottoman Empire and never regained his former influence Yet, his rivalry with Peter the Great had earned him fame in Europe; he was even depicted as a hero in numerous works of art. Lord Byron, Viktor Hugo Juliusz Slowacki wrote poems about Mazepa, while Piotr Tshaikovsky and Ferenc List dedicated musical works to him. After the Russian Empire won the Russian-Turkish war (1768-1774), it no longer needed the support of the Cossacks against the Crimean Khanate at its southern border. Empress Catherine II destroyed the Cossack settlement (Sich) and disbanded Cossack units. Most of them found refuge in the Ottoman Empire. This meant that Cossacks formally lost their recognition as an autonomous community in the Russian empire. 
Play video starting at :5:42 and follow transcript
5:42

Glossary and Further Readings

Glossary to video 3

Hetman Bohdan Khmelnytsky (1596-1657) — Hetman of the Zaporozhian Cossacks from 1648 to 1657 and leader of the revolt known as the Cossack Revolution in 1648-1657. Under his leadership, Cossack Ukraine was recognized as an autonomous territorial entity within the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth. Khmelnytsky conducted independent foreign policy and diplomatic relations with the neighboring countries and contributed to Cossack Ukraine becoming a protectorate of the Muscovite tsar.

Sylvester Kosiv (d. 1657) — a celebrated theologist, professor, and prefect at the Kyiv-Mohyla Academy, metropolitan of Kyiv in 1647-1657.

The Treaty of Zboriv — a peace treaty between Hetman Bohdan Khmelnytsky and Polish King John II Casimir signed near the town of Zboriv on 18 August 1649. The central provisions of the pact included the Polish king's affirmation of all Cossack rights and freedoms; expansion of the Cossack register to 40,000; transfer of Kyiv, Chernihiv, and Bratslav voivodeships to the Cossack administration headed by Hetman Khmelnytsky; and provision of the Orthodox church in Ukraine with the privileges compatible to those of the Catholic church in Poland. Kyivan metropolitan and two Orthodox bishops were to be granted places in the Polish Senate. The Polish Sejm was to consider the abolishment of the Church Union of Brest altogether.

The Pereyaslav Treaty (1654) — an alliance concluded by Hetman Bohdan Khmelnytsky and Muscovite Tsar Aleksei Mikhailovich. This treaty allowed Alexei Mikhailovich to reinforce his claims to the territory of Ukraine, which in this way acknowledged the tsar's protection. The original document of the treaty (if it ever existed) is missing. Several months later, the Cossack delegation to Moscow negotiated conditions of the protectorate (the so-called March Articles, the original copy of which did not survive). According to the draft copies, the freely elected hetman retained his right to conduct foreign policy except for the interaction with the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth and the Ottoman Empire. Moreover, the Cossacks executed full administrative control over Ukraine and preserved their army of 60 000 people. For the tsar’s protection, the Cossacks were to pay yearly tribute.

Hetman Ivan Mazepa (1639-1709) — a leader of Cossack Ukraine (1687-1709), which at that time was part of the Tsardom of Muscovy, and later — of the Russian Empire. Mazepa descended from the nobility of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth and graduated from the Kyiv-Mohyla Academy and the Jesuit college in Warsaw. He initially served at the court of Polish King John Casimir II. Later, Mazepa developed his career in the entourage of Cossack Hetman Petro Doroshenko. In 1687, he was elected the new hetman himself. In addition to his administrative talents, Mazepa was a distinguished patron of arts. Specifically, he donated huge amounts of money for the reconstruction of Orthodox churches in Kyiv. The artistic style of these reconstructions is now referred to as Mazepa Baroque. Mazepa strongly opposed the centralization policy of Peter the Great. So, he decided to support Swedish King Charles XII against Peter the Great in order to win greater autonomy for Ukraine. These plans failed after Mazepa and Charles XII were defeated in the Battle of Poltava in 1709.

The Battle of Poltava (1709) — the decisive victory of Peter I the Great of Russia over Charles XII of Sweden in the course of the Great Northern War. The battle destroyed Sweden’s status as the European great power and marked the beginning of Russian domination in Eastern Europe.

The Treaty of Pereyaslav is examined to demonstrate the complexities of Ukrainian-Russian relations, which have long-standing implications for Ukrainian sovereignty and national identity.
This concept is used to delve into the differing perspectives and frictions arising from the Pereyaslav Treaty, emphasizing the challenges in diplomatic agreements.
The inclusion of this conflict highlights the persistent external pressures on Ukraine and their impact on its quest for self-determination and stability during the 17th century.
This concept is explored to illustrate the fluctuating nature of Ukrainian autonomy within larger empires, providing context for its enduring aspirations for self-governance.
The battle is discussed to mark a turning point in Cossack fortunes and their socio-political structuring, preceding their disbandment by imperial powers.
The concept is introduced to explain the dissolution of the Cossack military structure, a critical event that influenced modern Ukrainian national consciousness and state-building.
This concept is key to understanding how historical events shaped the Ukrainian nation-building process, focusing on cultural and political revival post-Cossack era.

nodes.text: In 1648, charismatic Cossack hetman Bohdan Khmelnytsky turned this uprising into a large-scale rebellion, later called the Cossack Revolution. Importantly, Khmelnytsky secured the help of the Crimean Khan Islam III Geray from the very beginning. The first impressive victories of united Cossack & Crimean – near Zhovti Vody, Korsun’, Pyliavtsi and Zbarazh – proved them to be a serious challenge to the crown army.

On December 27th, 1648 Khmelnytsky triumphantly entered Kyiv He was greeted by the members of the Orthododx Church elite, led by the Metropolitan of Kyiv, Sylvestr Kosiv. This solemn reception cemented Khmelnytsky as the leader of the entire Orthodox community of the Polish Lithuanian Commonwealth.

The Treaty of Zboriv (1648) with the Polish king formally granted the Cossacks full control of three voivodeships Kyiv, Chernihiv and Pereyaslav though after their defeat near Berestechko (1651), their control was limited to the voivodeship of Kyiv only Thus, Khmelnytsky and his colonels made Ukrainian Cossacks the only frontier community in Central Eastern Europe to have developed into an autonomous political and territorial entity. Even though Khmelnytsky was formally appointed as a governor by the Polish king, he behaved as an independent ruler. He established diplomatic relations with the neighboring principalities of Moldavia and Wallachia, Transylvania Muscovy, and the Ottoman Empire.

Khmelnytsky perceived the treaty as a political contract between the Tsar and himself, where each party enjoyed its own rights and obligations. The Tsar, on the other hand, interpreted it as a unilateral act of his own will mandating unconditional incorporation of Ukraine into the Tsardom of Muscovy. This misunderstanding served as the reason for a number of conflicts between Cossack hetmans and Russian tsars over the second half of the 17th century and led to the Polish-Moscow war (1654-1667) As a result of the war, Ukraine was divided along the river Dnipro by the two rivals.

Ultimately, the controversy between tsars and hetmans culminated in the conflict between the Hetman Ivan Mazepa and the Tsar, Peter the Great. Well-educated Mazepa strived for the wide autonomy of his hetman office and maintained an exquisite court in his capital of Baturyn. While visiting the court, the ambassador from the French king Louis XV Jean de Baluze, noted that Mazepa apologized for his poor French, and spoke Polish and Latin to him while addressing his doctors in German and his architects in Italian. Mazepa, resentful of Peter the Great for neglecting his ‘obligations’ to the Cossacks, supported the tsar’s rival in the Great Northern war – the Swedish king Charles XII.

Unfortunately for Mazepa, the combined Cossack and Swedish army lost to Peter the Great in the Battle of Poltava (1709), which virtually put an end to the disputes about the autonomous status of the Ukrainian Cossacks within Russia. Hetman Mazepa fled to the Ottoman Empire and never regained his former influence Yet, his rivalry with Peter the Great had earned him fame in Europe; he was even depicted as a hero in numerous works of art. Lord Byron, Viktor Hugo Juliusz Slowacki wrote poems about Mazepa, while Piotr Tshaikovsky and Ferenc List dedicated musical works to him.

After the Russian Empire won the Russian-Turkish war (1768-1774), it no longer needed the support of the Cossacks against the Crimean Khanate at its southern border. Empress Catherine II destroyed the Cossack settlement (Sich) and disbanded Cossack units. Most of them found refuge in the Ottoman Empire. This meant that Cossacks formally lost their recognition as an autonomous community in the Russian empire.
However, in the 19th century, their success story as the creators of a semi-independent state in the Early-Modern period became an important nation-building factor for the emerging Ukrainian nation.
The State-Making Experience of the Ukrainian Cossacks
nodes.rationale: This assessment is designed to enable students to critically analyze the Cossacks' influence on Ukrainian state formation, encompassing their military, political, and cultural impact.

items: Beginning of the Ukrainian National Revival

0:13
The “long 19th century”, as it was called by British historian Eric Hobsbawm, was as long for Ukraine as it was for the rest of Europe. It began with the Napoleonic wars and ended with the First World War. I am Kateryna Dysa, a historian of the 18th and 19th centuries, and together, we'll explore Ukraine in the modern era. For Europe, the 19th century began with the so-called Napoleonic Wars. The vastly-promoted ideas of freedom national and popular sovereignty attracted many Europeans, even though these notions were a mere facade for imperial expansion. The interests of the stateless nations of the Russian Empire, such as Poles and Ukrainians, were not aligned, while Ukrainians remained loyal to the to the Russian tsar the Poles supported Napoleon, hoping that he would help them restore their lost state. After Napoleon’s army invaded the Russian Empire in 1812, Ukrainian Cossack regiments took part in the war against the French forces. 
Play video starting at :1:12 and follow transcript
1:12
This was a significant factor in the eventual victory of the French. Ivan Kotliarevsky – the founding father of Ukrainian literary language – was among those participating in the war against the French. In 1798, he published the first part of Eneyida, a brilliant parody of Virgil’s classical poem Aeneid. The main characters were turned into the Zaporozhian Cossacks and spoke vernacular Ukrainian. Why was it such a big deal? According to the new European cultural and intellectual Romanticism movement, language was one of the key aspects defining a nation. At the end of the 18th century, Сhurch Slavonic was still predominantly used as a literary language, not only by Ukrainians but also by Russians. 
Play video starting at :1:58 and follow transcript
1:58
Kotliarevsky’s promotion of literary Ukrainian language signaled the start of Ukrainian nation-building, which would continue for the next hundred years. In the 1836 Russification, which was a response to the rising Polish national movement, gave a nudge to the further development of Ukrainian national identity. After quelling the Polish uprising of 1830, the Russian authorities attempted to diminish Polish language and culture in the region, replacing them with Russian. In 1834, Kyiv University was established to replace the recently closed Polish educational institutions in Ukraine and Lithuania. It was intended to become an instrument for Russian acculturation. However, this temporary cultural vacuum gave Ukrainian culture a chance to flourish. By the end of the next decade, Kyiv had become the hotbed for new troubles of the Russian empire, this time connected with Ukrainian nationalism. 
Play video starting at :2:58 and follow transcript
2:58
In 1845, a group of Ukrainian intellectuals organized an underground association called the Brotherhood of St Cyril and Methodius. Its program included both cultural and political issues. Taras Shevchenko – a former serf, who became a painter and poet, was one of the members of the Brotherhood. In 1840, he published Kobzar, a collection of poems that made him famous. Shevchenko ideas were too radical for his time. He considered Ukraine to be a distinct nation worthy of freedom and independence. In 1847, the Brotherhood of St Cyril and Methodius was denounced, but the authorities did not take this seriously. most members were only mildly punished. 
Play video starting at :3:43 and follow transcript
3:43
Shevchenko’s satire of the Russian emperor, however, earned him the harshest sentence of the group. After his premature death in 1861, Shevchenko became a symbolic figure of modern Ukraine and a hero for the new generation of Ukrainian intellectuals - Ukrainophiles In the 1860s, they organized educational and cultural activities, such as theatrical plays and publications in Ukrainian and classes for peasants. These initiatives agitated the imperial authorities. In the wake of the Polish uprising of 1863, Ukrainophile activities were interpreted as a part of a Polish conspiracy. And as a result, the government issued a series of acts (dated 1863 and 1876) prohibiting virtually all publications in Ukrainian as well as banning the import of Ukrainian books. Theatrical performances in the Ukrainian language, where soon banned too. Thus, Ukrainian cultural life was hindered for more than a decade.

4:43

Glossary and Further Readings

Ukraine as part of the Russian Empire. After the gradual abolition of autonomy of the Hetmanate and Sloboda Ukraine in the second half of the eighteenth century, all the Ukrainian lands under the Russian rule became the subject of centralization policy in terms of imperial laws, administration, etc. By 1795, after the partitions of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, the Russian Empire annexed and incorporated its eastern (Ukrainian) territories: Podolia and Volhynia. The annexation of the Crimean Khanate by the Russian Empire in 1783 marked the beginning of the great colonization of south-eastern territories of Ukraine (so-called New Russia or Novorossia). In the next decades, these lands would be populated by Serbian, Greek, and Bulgarian refugees from the Ottoman Empire, religious dissenters from Germany, and people from other parts of Ukraine.

Ivan Kotliarevsky (1769-1838) — Ukrainian writer and activist, the founding father of Ukrainian literary language. Born in Poltava, he worked in his native city as a tutor and later served as a military officer during the Russian-Turkish war (1806-1812). Though Kotliarevsky retired in 1808, he formed the Cossack regiment in Poltava during the Napoleonic invasion of the Russian Empire. During the next decades, he served as a civic officer and, among other things, he was a director of the local theater in Poltava. Kotliarevsky’s key literary achievement is a burlesque poem Eneyidа (a parody of Vergil’s Aeneid) — the first literary work ever written in the modern Ukrainian language. The author turned Trojans who had to flee their native land into the Zaporozhian Cossacks who also lost their lands after Empress Catherine the Great ordered the destruction of the Zaporozhian Host at the end of the eighteenth century. The poem grasped many details of Ukrainian daily culture and traditions. Though the first parts of Eneyidа were published in 1798, the whole poem was published only after Kotliarevsky’s death in 1842.

Russification — a complex of policies and actions aimed to ensure the dominance of Russian language, culture, and identity in countries under Russian control. Russification measures were different for every nation. Two most notorious laws limiting the use of Ukrainian language were the Valuev Circular of 1863 and the Ems Ukaz of 1876. The Valuev Circular (named after the Minister of Internal Affairs Pyotr Valuev) forbade publication of religious texts and school textbooks in Ukrainian. The circular contained an infamous quote that “a separate Little Russian [Ukrainian] language has never existed, does not exist, and cannot exist”, and that it was just “Russian corrupted by Polish influence.” The further prohibition was imposed by the Ems Urkaz signed by Tsar Alexander II in Bad Ems, Germany. It prohibited all the publications in Ukrainian language, including song lyrics, as well as import of books in Ukrainian. The ban was temporarily lifted only in 1905.

The Brotherhood of Saints Cyril and Methodius (1845-1847) — a secret political society founded in Kyiv. The initiators of the Brotherhood included historian and writer Mykola Kostomarov, writer and ethnographer Panteleimon Kulish, journalist and public activist Vasyl Bilozerski, and others. In 1846, Ukrainian poet and artist Taras Shevchenko joined the organization. Their program envisioned broad Slavic confederation based on Christian morality and equality of nations. Some of the members stressed the importance of social agenda, such as the abolition of serfdom, while others did not support that idea. Most members agreed on the messianic role of Ukrainians in the future Slavic confederation. In 1847, the Brotherhood was denounced by one of its members, Oleksiy Petrov. The members of the society were arrested, and a brief investigation ensued. In result, the society’s activity was found non-threatening and the members of the Brotherhood were mildly punished.

Ukrainophiles — representatives of the Ukrainian nationalist movement in the Russian Empire in the 1860s-1880s. Ukrainophiles founded a broad network of educational societies called Hromady (Communities) in various cities of the Russian Empire from Saint Petersburg and Moscow to Kyiv and Odesa. They published several journals, including Osnova, and books for public schools. In the 1870s, Ukrainophiles were conducting archival and ethnographic work for Southwestern Branch of the Imperial Russian Geographical Society.

Taras Shevchenko (1814-1861) — a Ukrainian poet, writer, artist, and public figure. He is considered to be the founder of modern Ukrainian literature. Born as a serf, Shevchenko was bought out of serfdom in 1838. That same year, he was admitted to the Academy of Art in Saint Petersburg where he was awarded several prizes for his works. In 1840, his collection of poems Kobzar was published. During one of the visits to Ukraine, Shevchenko met with several members of the Brotherhood of Saints Cyril and Methodius. After the Brotherhood was denounced in 1847, Shevchenko was associated with it and arrested along with other members. His poem “The Dream” in which he mocked the emperor and his wife exposed him to the severest punishment — exile to serve military service as a private in a distant garrison. He was allowed to return to Ukraine only in 1859. In his poetry and other works, Shevchenko shows Ukrainians as a distinct nation which is separate from Russians and Poles. In his opinion, Ukraine with its long and complicated history deserves to be free and independent.

nodes.rationale: Introducing the concept of the Long 19th Century is essential to provide context for the major political, social, and cultural transformations that occurred in Ukraine, setting the stage for understanding its complex history of the 1800s and beyond.

The Napoleonic Wars are introduced as a pivotal event that influenced European political landscapes, including Ukraine, and served as a prerequisite to understanding the subsequent Ukrainian National Revival and nation-building efforts.

Ivan Kotliarevsky is introduced as a pioneering figure in Ukrainian literature, and his work is crucial for illustrating the emergence of a distinct Ukrainian cultural and literary identity during this period.

Eneyida, being the first literary work in the modern Ukrainian language, is used to elaborate Kotliarevsky's impact, highlighting how Ukrainian literature began to evolve with unique characteristics and national significance.

The Romanticism Movement is introduced to contextualize the cultural and intellectual shifts that promoted the use of vernacular languages and contributed to the Ukrainian National Revival, highlighting a broader European trend of the time.

Introducing Vernacular Ukrainian emphasizes the importance of language in cultural identity and nationalism, showing how movements like Romanticism encouraged the use of native languages as a form of cultural preservation and resistance.

Discussing Russification is crucial to understanding the challenges to Ukrainian identity and language, highlighting the tension between cultural assimilation processes and the preservation of Ukrainian identity.

The establishment of Kyiv University is introduced to underscore the role of educational institutions in fostering Ukrainian nationalism by providing the intellectual tools and environment necessary for political and cultural awakening.

Exploring Ukrainian Nationalism is essential for understanding the political and cultural awakening in Ukraine, shaped by earlier historical and cultural influences, demonstrating how these elements integrated into a cohesive national identity.

items: Ukrainians, Jews, and Crimean Tartars in the 19th Century

0:13
At the beginning of 1848, the revolutionary impulse from France reached even the distant corners of the multi-ethnic Austrian empire. This included the eastern provinces of Galicia and Bukovyna, which were populated mostly by Ukrainians, (then called Ruthenians). That year, local Ukrainians, supported by the governor of Galicia, established the Supreme Ruthenian Council, the first political organization in Lviv. Like the Ukrainians of the Russian Empire, Ukrainians of the Austrian Empire were also engaged in the process of nation building. But unlike their eastern counterparts, they were receiving support, at least temporarily, from the authorities who relied on Ukrainians to curb the much greater threat of Polish nationalism. The Ukrainians of Galicia and Bukovyna, the majority of whom were peasants, benefited from the reforms following the revolution of 1848, in particular the abolition of serfdom. As a result, peasants were empowered to participate in elections, and 25 deputies from these lands were elected to the Reichstag. In 1851 the Austrian authorities and Galician Poles eventually reached a compromise. Shortly there after the Supreme Ruthenian Council was abolished. 
Play video starting at :1:32 and follow transcript
1:32
Nevertheless, Ukrainians had already learned a lesson from their political experience, meaning the Ukrainian national awakening was already underway. Galicia and Bukovyna were a late addition to the Austrian empire and not a particularly highly valued one. Many aspects of economic and social life in these regions required reform and modernization. The emancipation of the Jewish population was one of the key issues to be resolved. As a result of the Edict of Tolerance of 1780 to Jews of the Austrian empire became "useful to the state". This meant that they were relieved of some restrictions and were allowed to attend schools and universities along with other citizens. It came at a cost, though they had to give up their native languages for the national languages of the empire. While Jews in the Austrian empire were gradually integrated into gentile society. 
Play video starting at :2:27 and follow transcript
2:27
They remained isolated in the Russian empire. Jews were kept away from the big cities and most of the Russian territory by limiting them to the so-called Pale of Settlement - designated territories of the former Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, within which Jews were allowed to settle mainly in small towns called shtetles. The situation had worsened by the end of the 19th century. The May Laws of 1882 introduced further restrictions. for example, Jews were excluded from legal practice and government jobs, and they were not allowed to vote or stand in elections for local councils. In 1871, Odesa became the center of the first modern pogrom, which would later become frequent events in other towns and cities. Crimean Tatars were another people who are not welcome in the Russian empire. After the annexation of Crimea in 1783, over 100,000 Tatars were forced to emigrate to the Ottoman Empire. 
Play video starting at :3:27 and follow transcript
3:27
Even more Tatars had to leave their houses on the fertile coastal lands and move to the dry steppe on the north of the peninsula during the Crimean War of 1853-1856 In the conflict between the Russian Empire on the one side and Britain and France on the other, Russians lost the war after several naval campaigns, Russians had to sign the humiliating Paris Peace Treaty. The disaster that befell the Russian Empire in Crimea demonstrated its economic and social backwardness. So, in the 1860sâ€“1870s, the new emperor launched a series of reforms which included the abolition of serfdom, judicial reform, the introduction of local self administration and army reform. In the meantime, Crimea, which in the 1870s has got its first railway, was attracting many Slav settlers. As a result, Crimean Tatars fell into the minority, constituting on this third of the population, traditional Tatar culture was diminished. It revived only towards the end of the century, thanks to the national movement initiated by the Tatars from the new class of intelligents

Glossary and Further Readings

Supreme Ruthenian Council (1848-1851) — the first Ukrainian political organization. The council was formed in Lviv, Galicia as the result of the Revolution of 1848 (also known as the Springtime of the Peoples or the Spring of Nations). The key purpose of the Supreme Ruthenian Council (SRC) was to support Ukrainians in the Austrian Empire through Ukrainian publications as well as introduction of Ukrainian language in educational institutions and local administration. They founded the first Ukrainian newspaper Zoria Halytska and supported the efforts of Ukrainian candidates during the parliamentary elections of 1848. The SRC insisted on the division of Galicia into Polish and Ukrainian parts, as they tried to prove that Ukrainians constituted a distinct nation. However, the partition never happened. The council was dissolved in 1851 on demand of the Austrian government.

The Pale of Settlement — a territory within the Russian Empire beyond which Jewish settlement was restricted. It appeared as a response to incorporation of the large proportion of the Jewish population after the partitions of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth. The Pale included such territories as Lithuania, Belarus, Ukraine, and Bessarabia, and almost 94 percent of all the Jewish population in the empire was concentrated there. However, Jews were also excluded from several major cities in this area, including Kyiv, Mykolaiv, Sevastopol, and Yalta. The Pale was abolished only after the February Revolution of 1917.

The May Laws of 1882 — laws officially titled as Temporary Regulations Concerning the Jews. Initially, these laws forbade Jews to settle and run business in villages, further limiting the territories they could occupy within the Pale of Settlement. Later, the new regulations were added, such as limitation of the number of Jews who could be admitted to schools and universities, strict regulation of Jews in legal profession, and ban on the participation of Jews in local elections. Though the laws were called “temporary,” they were abolished only after the February Revolution of 1917.

Pogroms in the Russian Empire were violent attacks against the local Jews, which were a manifestation of antisemitism. The first mass pogroms occurred in Odesa in 1821, 1859, and 1971. They mainly resulted from conflict between the local Greeks and Jews. However, the pogroms became frequent (more than 200) and grew in scale in the 1880s, after the assassination of Tsar Alexander II. Violent pogroms spanned many cities of the Pale of Settlement. The most notorious ones took place in Kyiv, Yelizavetgrad (now Kropyvnytskyi), Warsaw, and Odesa, resulting in many deaths. The pogroms continued in the next decades. One of the outcomes of the pogroms was mass emigration of the Jews to the West.

Crimean War (1853-1856) — the war between the Russian Empire on one side and the coalition of the British Empire, France, the Ottoman Empire, and others on the other side. Though the war was dubbed “Crimean,” it actually covered immense territories from the Baltic sea to the Far East. The Russian Empire planned to take away some Balkan territories of the Ottoman Empire with Orthodox population under the pretext of protecting them. After Russian Tsar Nicholas I refused to withdraw his army from the occupied Moldavia and Wallachia, the Ottoman Empire and its allies declared war on the Russian Empire. Afterwards, the fighting shifted to the Black Sea where the allies besieged the main base of the Russian fleet — Sevastopol — and even managed to partly occupy it in 1855. The war ended with the signing of the Treaty of Paris in 1856. According to the agreement, the sides exchanged lost territories and the Black Sea was declared neutral with both the Russian and the Ottoman empires losing their right to keep military fleets there.
