The liberation of serfs in the two empires stimulated a much-belated industrialization of Ukrainian lands, this resulted in rapid modernization, urbanization and social change. Sugar production was one of the first industries to develop in Ukraine since it depended on agricultural production of beets and involved peasants as the primary labor force. Modernization of the sugar industry in the second half of the century made this field quite profitable. Several families of entrepreneurs made their fortunes in sugar production and later spent part of their capital on philanthropy, supporting art and culture. The development of other sectors of modern industry and trade dependent on the construction of railroads. In the 1860s, the first railways were constructed in Western and Eastern Ukraine: Lviv-Peremyshl in Galicia and Odesa-Balta, respectively. It took almost 30 years to connect all major Ukrainian cities by the railroad lines. 
Play video starting at :1:11 and follow transcript
1:11
All these factors accelerated the colonisation of the steppe lands of south eastern Ukraine. Apart from agriculture, this region witnessed the development of two brand new, at least for Ukraine industries, coal mining and metallurgy. They attracted Western investments, mostly from French, Belgian and British bankers and from entrepreneurs, Welsh businessmen. John Hughes was one such entrepreneur. He arrived at the Azov steppe region, in the area of the Donets river basin, to establish metal works there, knowing that the region was rich in coal and iron ore. Thus, the late 1860s were the beginning of Ukraine’s industrial behemoth — the Donbas region renowned for its coal mines and metal, works not only during the imperial period but also throughout the Soviet and post-Soviet era. Unlike the Ukrainian lands of the Russian Empire, where there was a dynamic of industrialization, Galicia and Bukovyna remained the most economically stunted regions of the Habsburg empire By the end of the 19th century, they did not have any major industries. 
Play video starting at :2:19 and follow transcript
2:19
In the second half of the century, some regions of Galicia — like Boryslav — became oil extraction sites. However, the oil from Galicia was transported elsewhere in the empire for refinement. The same went for timber harvesting. Though Galicia provided the empire with timber, it never got its own wood processing industry. With no industry and no agricultural lands for liberated peasants, Galicians began to emigrate overseas en masse. This was in line with the broad tendency of the late 19th century. After all, steamship companies were offering relatively affordable price for cross-Atlantic travel. 
Play video starting at :3: and follow transcript
3:00
About 250,000 people from Galicia and Bukovyna left for Canada, the USA, Brazil, and Argentina. At the same time, landless peasants from the Russian Empire were going north, West but East. Their region of preference was Russia’s Far East The end of the 19th century saw a new tendency in the political life of Western Ukraine. By the end of the 1860s, Galician Ruthenians were no longer putting their hopes in the imperial government. Some of them — dubbed Moskvophiles or Russophiles — believed that they belonged to a larger Russian imperial nation, while others — Narodovtsis or Populists — considered themselves part of a larger Ukrainian nation. The latter had strong connections with the Ukrainophiles of the Russian Empire The Populists and Ukrainophiles were linked through various projects and activities and by the end of the century they envisioned a Ukrainian nation existing outside any imperial embrace. By the 1890s, the Galician populists had consolidated to the point where they could organize national parties to run for parliamentary elections. 
Play video starting at :4:12 and follow transcript
4:12
The most prominent parties where the Ruthenian Ukrainian Radical Party and the moderate Ukrainian National Democratic Party. Despite the many differences between them, one idea remained the same - the political independence of Ukraine. A similar militarization of the Ukrainian movement in the Russian empire became possible only after the Russian rev

Construction of Railroads as a concept is introduced in tandem with the Industrialization of Ukrainian Lands because it plays a crucial role in connecting urban centers and facilitating the colonization and economic development of the Steppe Lands.

This concept is introduced to highlight the role of external financial involvement in advancing the Industrialization of the Donbas, providing a nuanced view of economic development influenced by foreign capital.
This concept is introduced to frame the socio-economic disparities in Ukraine, setting the stage for examining emigration patterns and the search for better opportunities abroad.

Introduced as a method, the Industrialization of the Donbas highlights the detailed processes integral to regional economic development, drawing attention to local industries like coal mining and metallurgy as exemplars of broader industrial trends.

nodes.rationale: As an assessment, this element evaluates understanding of how industrial advancements intertwined with political movements, particularly the emergence of parties in Western Ukraine, assessing the student’s grasp of the complex socio-political landscape.

Industrialization of Ukrainian Lands is a central concept as it marks the economic transition influencing various sectors. It's introduced early to provide context for industrial transformations and related developments like the modernization of agriculture and infrastructure.

Colonisation of the Steppe Lands is presented as it demonstrates the impact of railroads on expanding agricultural frontiers, setting the stage for understanding spatial and economic transformations in 19th-century Ukraine.

lessons.title: Ukrainians Between Two Wars
items:
You may navigate through the transcript using tab. To save a note for a section of text press CTRL + S. To expand your selection you may use CTRL + arrow key. You may contract your selection using shift + CTRL + arrow key. For screen readers that are incompatible with using arrow keys for shortcuts, you can replace them with the H J K L keys. Some screen readers may require using CTRL in conjunction with the alt key

Play video starting at ::13 and follow transcript
0:13
World War I accelerated a long complicated transformation of ‘peasants – subjects of the European empires to nations and provoked revolutions that put an end to these empires. “Fourteen Points” speech by President Woodrow Wilson in 1918 legitimized the new world order that included national states created by European "small nations". My name is Natalia Shlikhta I study Soviet History and teach at the National University of Kyiv Mohyla Academy. Let’s explore Ukraine’s 20th century together. Ukrainians entered the war as part of warring armies on both sides of the Eastern Front At the end of the war, they made an attempt to unite all the Ukrainian territories into a sovereign national republic. Due to the inner discords among Ukrainian politicians and lack of support from the Allies, the Ukrainian state was not created then. As a result of postwar territorial arrangements, Ukrainian territories were divided between the newly emerging Soviet state, Poland, Czechoslovakia , and Romania The revolutionary events, commonly known as the Russian Revolution, started in February 1917 and spread all over the Romanov’s empire. 
Play video starting at :1:35 and follow transcript
1:35
The so-called Ukrainian Revolution of 1917-1921 was a complex set of events and processes: the proclamation of Ukrainian independence, the inner struggle of Ukrainian political forces and social emancipation of Ukrainian peasantry. It became even more complicated due to the Russian "reds" vs. "whites" struggle to reassemble the former imperial territory under the alternative slogans of ‘socialist revolution’ or ‘united Russia’ and also due to the interventions by the European great powers. Russian and Ukrainian Bolsheviks came out as ultimate winners of all these events. In 1922, the Union of the Soviet Socialist Republics was created by four republics Russian, Ukrainian, Belorussian and Transcaucasian According to the Agreement of December 29, the Soviet Union was seen as a federation of sovereign republics, each reserving their own right to leave the Union. The 1920s have seen relative, economic, national and cultural freedom in the Soviet state. Cultural liberty also included the freedom of individual artistic expression. Soviet nationalities policy of the period was distInctly contrasted to the imperial ‘prison of nations’. Use of national languages in educational and state institutions, recruitment of national cadres, and development of national cultures. 
Play video starting at :3:17 and follow transcript
3:17
All of these were considered necessary (yet temporary) concessions to non-Russian nations. Because of the the strong position of national communists in the party, Ukrainianization policy gained true momentum in the republic. At the time, Soviet Ukraine was seen as a new national Piedmont by the Ukrainians abroad. A short-lived Risorgimento of Ukrainian culture became possible thanks to Ukrainization policy. It was also part of a larger postwar European cultural scene. Poet Mykola Khvyliovy, artist Mykhailo Boichuk, theatrical director Les Kurbas, film director Oleksandr Dovzhenko, and others combined national motives and modernist experiments in their works Many of them gained worldwide recognition but were repressed at home. National and cultural development has abruptly ended with Stalin's "great turn" of 1929. 
Play video starting at :4:23 and follow transcript
4:23
Stalin aimed to build the socialist state as a mighty European military power. Accelerated industrialization, forced collectivization of agriculture and administrative centralization, were considered effective steps towards this ambitious aim. Because of his strategic position and rich natural resources, the Ukrainian republic became one of the strongholds of Soviet industrialization. The price of success was too high, though. The Great Famine of 1932-1933 was seen as an instrument to to suppress Ukrainian peasants’ opposition to the kolkhoz system. It took lives of millions of people The show trials against the so called “national counterrevolutionaries” in the early 1930s and the Great Terror repressed all the real and imaginary opposition within Ukrainian political and cultural elite. As a result, the Soviet Union entered World War II, being a totalitarian state with complete subordination of national republics to the Moscow center and totalitarian

1914-1939 Timeline and Further Readings

1914-1918 - World War I.

March 4, 1917 - The Central Council of Ukraine (the Central Rada) is set up in Kyiv following the February Revolution.

January 22, 1918 - The Central Rada declares the independence of the Ukrainian People's Republic.

1921 - Russian Red Army conquers two thirds of Ukraine. The Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic is established.

1921 - Galicia and Eastern Volhynia become part of Poland under the terms of the Peace of Riga signed by the Soviet and Polish governments.

1923-1931 - The Soviet government sets up the policy of korenizatsiya (putting down roots), or Ukrainization in case of Ukraine, that encourages national development of various domains of public life, such as education, publishing, government, and religion. However, the policy still imposes strict political limitations.

1924 - Mykola Khvylovy, a Ukrainian writer, publishes the novel 
I Am (Romance).

1926 - VAPLITE (Vilna Akademia Proletarskoi Literatury / Free Academy of Proletarian Literature) is established in Kharkiv. VAPLITE unites prominent young artists, such as Mykola Khvylovy, Mykhailo Yalovy, Oles Dosvitny, Mykola Kulish, Hryhorii Epik, Pavlo Tychyna, Ivan Senchenko, Oleksa Slisarenko, Petro Panch, Mykola Bazhan, Yuriy Yanovskyi, Yuriy Smolych, Ivan Dniprovsky, Oleksandr Kopylenko, etc.

1928 - The Shakhty Trial is the first major Soviet show trial. 53 engineers and managers from the town Shakhty are arrested after being accused of conspiring to sabotage the Soviet economy with the former owners of the coal mines.

1929 - Dziga Vertov, a Soviet director, who was born in Ukraine, presents his documentary film 
Man with a Movie Camera
, which was filmed in Kyiv, Odesa, and Moscow.

1930 - director Alexander Dovzhenko presents his film
 
Earth
, which is regarded as Dovzhenko's masterpiece. Both Earth and Man with a Movie Camera are famous and distinguished cinematography works.

March-April, 1930 - The show trial of the so-called Union for the Liberation of Ukraine. 45 Ukrainian intellectuals, politicians, writers, theologians, and a librarian are accused of anti-Soviet activities.

1932-1933 - Millions of people die during the man-made famine resulting from Stalin's collectivisation campaign, known as the Holodomor in Ukraine.(
The Great Famine Project by Harvard Ukrainian Research Institute
)

May 13, 1933 - Mykola Khvylovy commits suicide as a protest against Stalin’s repressions. In his suicide note, he writes:"Arrest of Yalovy - this is the murder of an entire generation ... For what? Because we were the most sincere Communists? I don't understand. The responsibility for the actions of Yalovy's generation lies with me, Khvylovy.Today is a beautiful sunny day. I love life - you can't even imagine how much. Today is the 13th. Remember I was in love with this number? Terribly painful.Long live communism.Long live the socialist construction.Long live the Communist Party.Mykola Khvylovy. 13. V. 1933"

1937-1938 - The Great Terror, a campaign of repression against politicians and party elite, academics, artists, musicians, writers, scientists, church members, national minorities, and others in the Soviet Union. Millions are sent to prison camps, which are located in the far reaches of Siberia or the Russian Far East. Hundreds of thousands die in Gulag prisons.

nodes.rationale: Introducing World War I sets the stage for understanding the geopolitical rearrangements and the emergence of national self-determination principles that lead to events like the February Revolution and the Ukrainian Revolution of 1917-1921.

The February Revolution is included as a pivotal event that dismantled the Tsarist regime and triggered subsequent political changes, laying the groundwork for revolutionary movements within Ukraine, which are essential for understanding Ukraine's tumultuous journey towards independence.
This concept is introduced to help students comprehend its profound impact on Ukraine's political landscape, as it created a power vacuum that various Ukrainian political entities sought to exploit.

This concept serves as a detailed exploration of Ukraine's quest for self-determination amidst the larger Russian revolutionary movements, providing a critical understanding of Ukraine's national identity formation during this period.

nodes.text: Union of the Soviet Socialist Republics
nodes.rationale: Discussing the formation of the USSR provides context for the political environment Ukraine found itself in, influencing subsequent policies like Korenizatsiya and Ukrainianization.

Introducing this policy helps elucidate the USSR's approach to managing its diverse ethnic groups, which directly affected Ukraine's cultural and political policies, particularly during the Soviet era.

Presented to illustrate the Soviet attempts at promoting local languages and cultures, Ukrainianization is crucial for understanding shifts in cultural liberty and identity within Ukraine during the early Soviet period.
Cultural Liberty is discussed to highlight how policies like Ukrainianization aimed to encourage ethnic expression and the paradox of liberalization under a centralized authoritarian regime.
This concept marks a significant shift in Soviet policies towards rapid industrialization and collectivization, impacting Ukraine's social and economic structure.
Included to demonstrate the aggressive economic changes under Stalin, students can evaluate its profound impacts on Ukraine's infrastructure and economy during the early USSR era.
This method reveals the drastic changes imposed on Ukrainian agriculture, directly connecting to the hardships and famine faced by the Ukrainian population during Stalin's regime.
Introduced to explain the political repression and purges during Stalin's era, allowing students to critically assess the broader impact on Ukrainian social structures and politics.
This concept highlights the attempt by Ukrainian leaders to establish a national government, crucial for understanding the national movements during the Ukrainian Revolution of 1917-1921.

nodes.text: Declaration of Independence of the Ukrainian People's Republic
nodes.rationale: This declaration is detailed to emphasize its role in the Ukrainian Revolution of 1917-1921, marking Ukraine's move towards asserting sovereignty and national identity.

nodes.text: Russian Red Army Conquers Two Thirds of Ukraine
nodes.rationale: Included to show the geopolitical struggles and military confrontations impacting Ukrainian sovereignty during the revolution, essential for understanding subsequent Soviet control over Ukraine.
An essential concept for recognizing Ukraine's status within the USSR, it sets the stage for understanding subsequent Soviet policies and their impacts on Ukraine.
This treaty concluded a pivotal conflict involving Poland and Soviet Russia, affecting Ukrainian territory and sovereignty, crucial for comprehending post-war borders and influences.
Discussed as a Soviet policy intended to promote national cultures within USSR republics, it provides context for the temporary support of Ukrainian language and culture under Soviet rule.
Introduced to illustrate the show trials that signaled the beginning of Stalin's political purges, providing insight into the mechanisms of control under the Soviet regime.
These trials are included to analyze the repression strategies used to eliminate perceived threats to Soviet control, offering students insights into Stalin's political strategies.

nodes.text: The Ukrainian territories were divided between the Soviet state, Poland, Czechoslovakia, and Romania as a result of postwar territorial arrangements.
The Russian "reds" vs. "whites" struggle complicated the situation due to their desire to reassemble the former imperial territory under slogans of 'socialist revolution' or 'united Russia'.
The union created by the agreement of December 29, 1922, represented a federation of sovereign republics, each of which retained the right to leave the Union.
A short-lived cultural renaissance in Ukraine, known as the Piedmont by Ukrainians abroad, occurred as part of a larger postwar European cultural scene due to the Ukrainianization policy.
Stalin's ambition was to transform the socialist state into a powerful European military power, leading to industrialization and agricultural collectivization in Ukraine, but at a high cost.
Stalin's policies resulted in the Great Famine, intended to suppress Ukrainian peasant opposition to the kolkhoz system, causing millions of deaths.
The show trials of the early 1930s targeted real and perceived opposition from Ukrainian political and cultural elites, enabling totalitarian control.

In March-April 1930, 45 Ukrainian intellectuals, politicians, writers, theologians, and a librarian were accused of anti-Soviet activities during the show trial of the Union for the Liberation of Ukraine.

The Great Terror from 1937 to 1938 was a campaign of severe repression affecting politicians, academics, artists, and many others across the Soviet Union, leading to widespread imprisonment and death.

nodes.rationale: This assessment is introduced to evaluate students' understanding of Ukraine's complex political and cultural shifts between World War I and World War II, encouraging critical thinking about the period's challenges and transformations.

Presented as a fundamental piece shaping post-World War I geopolitics, it influenced Eastern European nations' self-determination, crucial for contextualizing the political aspirations in Ukraine during this era.

lessons.title: A Way to Independence
items:
You may navigate through the transcript using tab. To save a note for a section of text press CTRL + S. To expand your selection you may use CTRL + arrow key. You may contract your selection using shift + CTRL + arrow key. For screen readers that are incompatible with using arrow keys for shortcuts, you can replace them with the H J K L keys. Some screen readers may require using CTRL in conjunction with the alt key

Play video starting at ::13 and follow transcript
0:13
Just as during World War I, Ukrainians were divided when entering the Second World War. Political borders between them almost disappeared in 1939-1940 when Galicia, Eastern Volhynia, Bessarabia, and and Northern Bukovyna became parts of Soviet Ukraine Ideological divisions among Ukrainians remain, though. The vast majority of them fought against Wehrmacht being a part of the Red Army. Others saw the Soviet German war as an opportunity to regain the Ukrainian independence. They organized a large scale nationalist guerrilla movement under the umbrella of the Ukrainian insurgent army. Altogether Ukrainians were just another "small nation" in the region that found itself in between "two totalitarianism". The Ukrainian population was also among those who suffered the most. 
Play video starting at :1:10 and follow transcript
1:10
Civilians suffered from the both armies. Many Jews perished during the Holocaust, while all the Crimean Tatars were deported to the desert areas of Central Asia, being accused of collaboration with the Nazis. Ukrainian settlements from small villages to major cities were completely devastated. History is written by the victors. At first glance, Ukrainians were benefactors of Yalta and Potsdam Conferences In 1945, Transcarpathia was incorporated to Soviet Ukraine, thus uniting all of the Ukrainian ethnic territory under one state. Moreover, Soviet Ukraine was given a right for semi-independent external representation. It even became one of 51 co-founders of the United Nations Organization. 
Play video starting at :2:2 and follow transcript
2:02
However, inside the country, which was rebuilding after the war destruction, the Stalinist terror was renewed. Ukrainians, Poles and Jews became victims of the post-war deportations. From then on, new persecutions were justified on the basis of the Cold War. The so-called “enemies of the Soviet regime ” – from believers in God to non-conformist intelligentsia were repressed for allegedly being “fascists”, “bourgeois nationalists,” and “West-supporters.” The death of Stalin in 1953 and Nikita Khrushchev speech about Stalin's personality cult in 1956 opened a new page in Soviet history. Khrushchev’s policy of de-Stalinization presumed the diminishment of repressions, economic reforms, social and cultural liberalization, and even transfer of certain political powers to the republican level. But all these reforms remain inconsistent and incomplete. 
Play video starting at :3:5 and follow transcript
3:05
The leader of Ukrainian communists Petro Shelest stood by the republic’s economic interests and national culture in front of the Moscow center. His successor - Volodymyr Shcherbytsky - supported Brezhnev’s policy of re-centralization, national assimilation, and Russification. The latter was carried out under the slogan of the “creation of the new Soviet people ”. Khrushchev’s policy of liberalization gave birth to a new generation of Soviet intelligentsia, known as the Sixtiers A desire for individual freedom united them with the generation of 1968. But they formed realisation of the ideas in artistic activity rather than open protests. Some of them – such as film director Sergei Parajanov and artist Alla Horska – gained worldwide recognition The Ukrainian political cinema of the 1960s introduced national motives and went beyond the canons of social realism. Ukrainian became the language of Soviet popular music, with the songs written by Volodymyr Ivasiuk. The mid-1960s also saw the birth of the dissent movement in the Soviet Union. 
Play video starting at :4:31 and follow transcript
4:31
In a decade, it evolved into a human rights movement in support of the Helsinki Accords. Dissenters in the republic formed Ukrainian Helsinki Group in 1976. They defended the national, religious and civil rights of Ukrainians, Jews and Crimean Tatars. They also appealed to the international community, which was a completely new phenomenon during the Cold War. Almost all of them were repressed. At the same time, the majority of the Ukrainian population continued to rely on the paternalistic state that supported and controlled them throughout their lives. Such was the late Soviet civilisation. 
Play video starting at :5:17 and follow transcript
5:17
The crisis inside the country became evident in the early 1980s. It only got worse because of strengthening of centrifugal movements in the republics, particularly in the Baltic states and Ukraine. The authorities’ unreadiness to implement true reforms - political, economic, and social - became obvious during Gorbachev's perestroika. The Chernobyl accident of 1986 became a symbol of Soviet stagnation and inability to reform the system. The loss in the arms race of the Cold War and the disintegration of the socialist camp in 1989 suggests additional factors of the Soviet Union's collapse. The independence of Ukraine was proclaimed by the Republican parliament on August 24, 1991. Over 90% of those who participated in the national referendum on December 1st supported this decision. 
Play video starting at :6:19 and follow transcript
6:19
National anthem Glorious spirit of Ukraine shines and lives forever Blessed by Fortune brotherhood will stand up together.

1940-1990 Timeline and Further Readings

1939-1945 - World War II.

1939 - Galicia and Eastern Volhynia are annexed by the Soviet Union under the terms of the Nazi-Soviet Pact.

1940 - The Soviet Union annexes Northern Bukovina and Southern Bessarabia (then parts of Romania), which is confirmed by the Paris Peace Treaties in 1947.

1944 - Deportation of 200,000 Crimean Tatars to Central Asia following false accusations of collaboration with Nazi Germany.

1945 - Transcarpathia becomes part of Ukraine under the treaty between Soviet Union and Czechoslovakia.

1947 - Operation Vistula, forced resettlement of the Ukrainian minority from the south-eastern provinces of Poland to the Recovered Territories in the west of the country.

1953 - Beginning of the Khrushchev Thaw — a series of liberal political reforms. One of the most important parts of the Thaw is de-Stalinization — a process of denouncing the cult of personality of Stalin and eliminating the consequences of his regime. It starts after Stalin’s death with Khrushchev's "Secret Speech" at the 20th Congress of the Communist Party, in which he accuses Stalin of collaborating with Hitler, conducting terror and mass crimes from the 1930s to the 1950s as well as imposing measures to build a cult of personality of the dictator.

The Thaw allows some freedom of information in the media, arts, and culture.

1954 - Nikita Khrushchev transfers the Crimean peninsula to Ukraine.

1959 - Levko Lukyanenko creates the Ukrainian Workers’ and Peasants’ Union, the first political underground organization in post-war Ukraine. The ultimate goals of the organization are the secession of Ukraine from the USSR by means of a referendum and the creation of a sovereign Ukrainian state.

1961 - KGB arrests Levko Lukyanenko and other members of the Ukrainian Workers’ and Peasants’ Union. Levko Lukyanenko is sentenced to death.

1965 - Premiere of the film Shadows of Forgotten Ancestors by 
Sergei Parajanov 
in Kyiv, during which literary critic Ivan Dziuba, poet Vasyl Stus, and journalist Vyacheslav Chornovil protest against the arrests of Ukrainian intellectuals on the stage.

1965 - Ivan Dzuba publishes Internationalism or Russification?, in which he analyzes the national and cultural policy of the Soviet Union in Ukraine from a Marxist perspective.

1976 - Creation of the Ukrainian Helsinki Group, the goal of which is to monitor the Soviet Government's compliance with the Helsinki Accords, which ensure human rights. Most members of the Ukrainian Helsinki Group, such as Levko Lukyanenko, Vitaly Kalynychenko, Vyacheslav Chornovil, Olha Heyko, Vasyl Stus, and Mykola Rudenko, are arrested.

1985 - 
Vasyl Stus
 is nominated for the Nobel prize, but he dies before the nomination materializes in forced labor camp, where he is sent for anti-Soviet activity.1987- Glasnost, a policy of openness, transparency, and open discussion of political and social issues, is instituted by Mikhail Gorbachev. Glasnost is associated with other generalized concepts, such as perestroika (restructuring) which started in 1985 and demokratizatsiya (democratization).

April 26, 1986 - A reactor at the Chernobyl nuclear power station explodes, sending a radioactive plume across Europe.

1989 - The People's Movement of Ukraine for Reconstruction is organized. Ukrainian poet and screenwriter Ivan Drach is elected as the first leader of the movement. Mykhailo Horyn, Vyacheslav Chornovil, and others also participate in the initiative. In 1990, the movement proclaims its main objectives — the independence of Ukraine and establishment of a democratic state.

1990 - Human chain from Lviv to Kyiv for the 71st anniversary of the Reunion Day (Act Zluky) of the Ukrainian People's Republic and the West Ukrainian People’s Republic.

July 16, 1990 - The Declaration of State Sovereignty of Ukraine.

Octoder, 1990 - The so-called Revolution on Granite, a student protest campaign against the results of the March 1990 parliamentary election in Ukraine.

August 24, 1991 - The Act of Declaration of Independence of Ukraine.
